sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 28-year-old female, presents with a chronic history of asthma symptoms, including coughing and wheezing, which have been persistent for the past six months.
She reports using her inhaler, Ventolin (albuterol), almost daily, with a frequency of two puffs every four to six hours as needed.
She has had multiple exacerbations, requiring urgent care visits and emergency department admissions, with the last one being a month ago.
She mentions that her asthma symptoms worsen when she is exposed to cats, as evidenced by a flare-up after visiting her aunt's house.
She also reports using antibiotics and steroids, including a prednisone taper pack, as prescribed by her primary care provider or urgent care provider.
She denies any history of smoking, vaping, or marijuana use.
Past medical history: The patient has a past history of asthma, with a single hospitalization at the age of 8 years old, but no subsequent hospitalizations since then.
Review of systems: No other symptoms in other organs are mentioned.
Current medications: Ventolin (albuterol) 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed; no other medications are mentioned.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure is 128/82, respiratory rate is 16, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
Physical exam: The patient has diminished lung sounds throughout with occasional slight expiratory wheeze bilaterally.
The neck is supple, trachea is midline, and there is no lymphadenopathy.
The patient has strong bilateral pulses and no clubbing on any fingertips.
Capillary refill is brisk.
Test Results: Pulmonary function test results are consistent with asthma.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are consistent with moderate intermittent asthma.
Problem list: Asthma; confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms one puff twice per day to help prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for rescue inhaler use.
The patient can continue using Ventolin (albuterol) 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed.
I will put together an asthma action plan to help manage symptoms and identify triggers.
The patient will be seen in 30 days for follow-up.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen in 30 days for follow-up, or sooner if symptoms worsen.
